patterns.what_to_look_for: Introspection (__schema/__type) enabled on a production or otherwise sensitive endpoint, leaking the full type system, hidden mutations, and internal field names that should not be discoverable by unauthenticated clients.
source_indicators: ApolloServer({ introspection: true }) or introspection left default-on in prod config
graphql-yoga / express-graphql with graphiql: true or no NODE_ENV gate around introspection
Missing NoSchemaIntrospectionCustomRule / validationRules in schema setup
Hasura/PostGraphile defaults with no introspection lockdown env var set
blackbox_indicators: POST {"query":"{__schema{types{name}}}"} returns 200 with a populated types array
query on __type(name:...) returns field definitions including @deprecated/internal fields
Error messages suggest fields via 'Did you mean ...' even when introspection appears off
GraphiQL/Apollo Sandbox HTML served at the GraphQL path
evidence_required: Full request/response for the __schema introspection query showing returned type/field names
Endpoint URL and environment classification (prod vs staging)
Auth context of the requester (unauthenticated vs low-priv)
false_positive_checks: Confirm the endpoint is production/sensitive, not an intentionally-open staging/docs sandbox
Check whether a WAF/edge gates introspection by IP/header before flagging
Distinguish full introspection from a curated public schema (federated gateway) that is intended
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send a single POST with query {__schema{queryType{name} types{name}}} and record whether type names are returned.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as info-disclosure enabler; rate higher when introspection reveals admin-only mutations or PII-bearing types, and pair with any downstream authz finding it unlocked.

patterns.what_to_look_for: A resolver that returns data without checking the caller's identity/role, relying on the GraphQL layer being 'internal' or on the client to not call it — broken object-/operation-level authorization at the resolver boundary.
source_indicators: Resolver function bodies that call the data layer (db.user.find, prisma.x.findMany) with no context.user / ctx.auth check
Authorization only enforced in REST controllers but the GraphQL resolver hits the same model directly
@authenticated/@hasRole directive defined but not applied to sensitive Query/Mutation fields
context builder that never populates user identity, or resolvers ignoring context entirely
blackbox_indicators: Sensitive query (e.g. users, invoices, apiKeys) returns data with no/low-priv token
Same query returns 200 with data for user A's resources when only user B is authenticated
No 'not authorized' error path observed across privileged fields
evidence_required: Request with attacker auth context returning another principal's or admin-only data
Identification of the resolver/field and confirmation no authz check exists in its path
A baseline request showing the intended authorized response for contrast
false_positive_checks: Verify authz is not enforced one layer down (data-loader/service guard, RLS in DB)
Confirm the returned data is actually sensitive and not intentionally public
Check a global middleware/plugin isn't already rejecting before resolver runs
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With a low-privilege token, request one sensitive field and compare the returned scope against that account's entitlements.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as broken authorization with the specific field path; severity scales with data sensitivity and whether it crosses tenant/user boundaries.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Object-level access works but a specific sensitive FIELD on an otherwise-authorized type is not protected — e.g. you may read your own User node but the resolver also exposes ssn/passwordHash/role on any User you can reference.
source_indicators: Type-level guard present but individual field resolvers (User.email, User.role, User.internalNotes) have no per-field check
Field returned straight from the ORM row spread (return {...user}) exposing all columns
Sensitive fields lack a @auth/field-level directive while the parent type has one
GraphQL schema marks a field non-null/queryable that maps to a privileged DB column
blackbox_indicators: Selecting a sensitive subfield (e.g. { user(id:X){ role internalNotes } }) returns it for non-owned/non-admin context
Field returns real value for low-priv user where it should be null/redacted
Differential: object returns 200 but only the sensitive field is over-exposed
evidence_required: Query selecting the specific sensitive field and the response containing its value
Field path and the resolver/column it maps to
Caller authorization level showing they should not see that field
false_positive_checks: Confirm the field value is real, not a placeholder/masked stub
Verify the field isn't legitimately public for that role
Rule out the value being the caller's own (owner-readable) data
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Query an authorized object but add one sensitive subfield and check whether its value is returned for the current role.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as field-level authorization gap naming the exact field; emphasize that object-level checks pass so it evades coarse access reviews.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Query batching and/or field aliasing let a single HTTP request multiply work — used to bypass per-request rate limits, brute force (login/OTP/coupon) via aliased mutations, or amplify cost.
source_indicators: Server accepts JSON-array batched operations (graphql-express/apollo batch=true) with no per-operation throttle
Rate limiting applied per HTTP request, not per GraphQL operation/field
No alias-count or duplicate-field limit; no graphql-no-alias / cost plugin
Login/verify/redeem mutation reachable repeatedly without lockout state keyed server-side
blackbox_indicators: POST of a JSON array of operations returns an array of results (batching enabled)
Single request with 100+ aliased copies of the same mutation each execute (e.g. login_1..login_100)
Rate-limit/lockout counters do not increment per aliased attempt
Response time/size scales linearly with alias count
evidence_required: Request containing N aliased operations and a response showing N independent results
Proof a protection (rate limit/lockout/OTP attempt counter) was bypassed across the aliases
Count of operations executed in the single request
false_positive_checks: Confirm aliases actually execute independently rather than being deduped/short-circuited
Check a cost/depth/alias-limit plugin doesn't reject above a threshold
Ensure the targeted mutation has security-relevant per-attempt state (else it is only cost amplification)
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send one request with ~20 harmlessly-aliased copies of a benign query and confirm all aliases resolve independently.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as rate-limit/brute-force bypass when a security counter is defeated; otherwise as DoS-amplification. Cite the alias/batch count achieved.

patterns.what_to_look_for: No query depth or complexity limit, so a deeply nested/cyclic query (via reciprocal relations like author->posts->author) or a high-multiplier query can exhaust CPU/DB and cause denial of service.
source_indicators: Schema with bidirectional relations and no graphql-depth-limit / graphql-query-complexity / costAnalysis plugin
validationRules array missing depthLimit/complexity rules
Per-field DB calls (N+1) with no DataLoader and no complexity cap
No timeout/pagination cap on list resolvers (first/limit unbounded)
blackbox_indicators: Deeply nested cyclic query returns 200 instead of a 'query exceeds maximum depth' error
Response latency grows sharply with nesting depth or requested list size
No complexity/cost error returned for an obviously expensive query
Unbounded first:/last: pagination accepted
evidence_required: A nested/cyclic query and the server response (success + measured latency, or absence of a depth error)
Evidence of resource impact (response-time delta vs baseline) without taking the service down
false_positive_checks: Confirm no depth/complexity/timeout guard rejects beyond a threshold (test increasing depths)
Distinguish real backend cost from a cheap cached/short-circuited path
Stop at benign latency demonstration — do not actually exhaust the target
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Submit a moderately nested cyclic query (e.g. depth ~10) and record whether a depth/complexity limit error is returned vs a 200.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as DoS-via-query-complexity; quantify with a benign latency-scaling curve and recommend depth+complexity limits, never demonstrate an actual outage.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Relay-style global node(id:) lookup or any id-argument resolver that decodes an opaque/base64 global ID and fetches by primary key without an ownership/tenant check — classic IDOR through the GraphQL node interface or mutation inputs.
source_indicators: node(id) resolver / fromGlobalId(id) that does db.findByPk(decodedId) with no caller-scope filter
Mutation inputs taking a raw id (deletePost(id), updateOrder(id)) resolving without verifying ownership
Sequential/base64-of-'Type:123' IDs decoded then trusted
Authorization keyed on the id argument value rather than on context.user owning the object
blackbox_indicators: node(id:"<base64 Type:OTHER_ID>") returns another tenant's/user's object
Incrementing or re-encoding a global ID yields objects the caller does not own
Mutation referencing a non-owned id succeeds (200, no authz error)
IDs are predictable base64 of 'Type:<int>'
evidence_required: Request with a global ID for a non-owned object and a response returning that object's data (or a successful mutation)
Decoding of the global ID showing it maps to a foreign primary key/tenant
Caller auth context proving lack of entitlement to that object
false_positive_checks: Verify the object is genuinely owned by a different principal/tenant, not shared/public
Confirm no row-level-security or post-fetch ownership filter silently scopes results
Ensure the returned id isn't the attacker's own re-encoded id
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Decode your own global node id, re-encode it for an adjacent id, and read-only query node(id) to check if a foreign object is returned.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as IDOR/BOLA via global node id; show the decode->cross-object read and note it generalizes to every id-taking mutation lacking ownership checks.
